* [Lincoln University, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(Pennsylvania) "Lincoln University (Pennsylvania)") ([BA](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts")) * [Howard University](/wiki/Howard_University "Howard University") ([LLB](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Laws "Bachelor of Laws"))

"**Thurgood**" **Marshall** (July 2, 1908 – January 24, 1993) was an American [civil rights lawyer](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") and [jurist](/wiki/Jurist "Jurist") who served as an [associate justice of the Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Associate_justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Associate justice of the Supreme Court of the United States") from 1967 until 1991. He was the Supreme Court's first [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") justice. Before his judicial service, he was an attorney who fought for civil rights, leading the [NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund](/wiki/NAACP_Legal_Defense_and_Educational_Fund "NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund"). Marshall was a prominent figure in the movement to end [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") in American public schools. He won 29 of the 32 civil rights cases he argued before the Supreme Court, culminating in the Court's landmark 1954 decision in *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*, which rejected the [separate but equal doctrine](/wiki/Separate_but_equal_doctrine "Separate but equal doctrine") and held segregation in public education to be unconstitutional. President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") appointed Marshall to the Supreme Court in 1967. A staunch liberal, he frequently dissented as the Court became increasingly conservative.

**Thoroughgood** "**Thurgood**" **Marshall** (July 2, 1908 – January 24, 1993) was an American [civil rights lawyer](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") and [jurist](/wiki/Jurist "Jurist") who served as an [associate justice of the Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Associate_justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Associate justice of the Supreme Court of the United States") from 1967 until 1991. He was the Supreme Court's first [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") justice. Before his judicial service, he was an attorney who fought for civil rights, leading the [NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund](/wiki/NAACP_Legal_Defense_and_Educational_Fund "NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund"). Marshall was a prominent figure in the movement to end [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") in American public schools. He won 29 of the 32 civil rights cases he argued before the Supreme Court, culminating in the Court's landmark 1954 decision in *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*, which rejected the [separate but equal doctrine](/wiki/Separate_but_equal_doctrine "Separate but equal doctrine") and held segregation in public education to be unconstitutional. President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") appointed Marshall to the Supreme Court in 1967. A staunch liberal, he frequently dissented as the Court became increasingly conservative.

Born in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, Marshall attended [Lincoln University](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(Pennsylvania) "Lincoln University (Pennsylvania)") and the [Howard University School of Law](/wiki/Howard_University_School_of_Law "Howard University School of Law"). At Howard, he was mentored by [Charles Hamilton Houston](/wiki/Charles_Hamilton_Houston "Charles Hamilton Houston"), who taught his students to be "social engineers" willing to use the law to fight for civil rights. Marshall opened a law practice in Baltimore but soon joined Houston at the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP") in New York. They worked together on the segregation case of *[Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada](/wiki/Missouri_ex_rel._Gaines_v._Canada "Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada")*; after Houston returned to Washington, Marshall took his place as special counsel of the NAACP, and he became director-counsel of the newly formed NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund. He participated in numerous landmark Supreme Court cases involving civil rights, including *[Smith v. Allwright](/wiki/Smith_v._Allwright "Smith v. Allwright")*, *[Morgan v. Virginia](/wiki/Morgan_v._Virginia "Morgan v. Virginia")*, *[Shelley v. Kraemer](/wiki/Shelley_v._Kraemer "Shelley v. Kraemer")*, *[McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents](/wiki/McLaurin_v._Oklahoma_State_Regents "McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents")*, *[Sweatt v. Painter](/wiki/Sweatt_v._Painter "Sweatt v. Painter")*, *Brown*, and *[Cooper v. Aaron](/wiki/Cooper_v._Aaron "Cooper v. Aaron")*. His approach to desegregation cases emphasized the use of sociological data to show that segregation was inherently unequal.

In 1961, President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") appointed Marshall to the [U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/U.S._Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit"), where he favored a broad interpretation of constitutional protections. Four years later, Johnson appointed him as the [U.S. Solicitor General](/wiki/U.S._Solicitor_General "U.S. Solicitor General"). In 1967, Johnson nominated Marshall to replace Justice [Tom C. Clark](/wiki/Tom_C._Clark "Tom C. Clark") on the Supreme Court; despite opposition from [Southern senators](/wiki/Southern_senators "Southern senators"), he was confirmed by a 69–11 vote. He was often in the majority during the consistently liberal [Warren Court](/wiki/Warren_Court "Warren Court") period, but after appointments by President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") made the Court more conservative, Marshall frequently found himself in dissent. His closest ally on the Court was Justice [William J. Brennan Jr.](/wiki/William_J._Brennan_Jr. "William J. Brennan Jr."), and the two voted the same way in most cases.

Marshall's jurisprudence was pragmatic and drew on his real-world experience. His most influential contribution to constitutional doctrine, the "sliding-scale" approach to the [Equal Protection Clause](/wiki/Equal_Protection_Clause "Equal Protection Clause"), called on courts to apply a flexible [balancing test](/wiki/Balancing_test "Balancing test") instead of a more rigid [tier-based analysis](/wiki/Tiered_scrutiny "Tiered scrutiny"). He fervently opposed the [death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States"), which in his view constituted [cruel and unusual punishment](/wiki/Cruel_and_unusual_punishment "Cruel and unusual punishment"); he and Brennan dissented in more than 1,400 cases in which the majority refused to review a death sentence. He favored a robust interpretation of the [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") in decisions such as *[Stanley v. Georgia](/wiki/Stanley_v._Georgia "Stanley v. Georgia")*, and he supported abortion rights in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* and other cases. Marshall retired from the Supreme Court in 1991 and was replaced by [Clarence Thomas](/wiki/Clarence_Thomas "Clarence Thomas"). He died in 1993.

## Early life and education

Thurgood[[a]](#cite_note-2) Marshall was born on July 2, 1908, in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, to Norma and William Canfield Marshall.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 30, 35 His father held various jobs as a waiter in hotels, in clubs, and on railroad cars, and his mother was an elementary school teacher.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 41, 45 The family moved to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") in search of better employment opportunities not long after Thurgood's birth; they returned to Baltimore when he was six years old.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 50 He was an energetic and boisterous child who frequently found himself in trouble.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 37 Following legal cases was one of William's hobbies, and Thurgood oftentimes went to court with him to observe the proceedings.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 37 Marshall later said that his father "never told me to become a lawyer, but he turned me into one ... He taught me how to argue, challenged my logic on every point, by making me prove every statement I made, even if we were discussing the weather."[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 38

Marshall attended the Colored High and Training School (later [Frederick Douglass High School](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass_High_School_(Baltimore,_Maryland) "Frederick Douglass High School (Baltimore, Maryland)")) in Baltimore, graduating in 1925 with honors.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 69, 79[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 34 He then enrolled at [Lincoln University](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(Pennsylvania) "Lincoln University (Pennsylvania)") in [Chester County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Chester_County,_Pennsylvania "Chester County, Pennsylvania"), the oldest college for African Americans in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 43 The mischievous Marshall was suspended for two weeks in the wake of a [hazing](/wiki/Hazing "Hazing") incident, but he earned good grades in his classes and led the school's debating team to numerous victories.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 43–44, 46 His classmates included the poet [Langston Hughes](/wiki/Langston_Hughes "Langston Hughes").[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 88 Upon his graduation with honors in 1930 with a bachelor's degree in American literature and philosophy,[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 46 Marshall—being unable to attend the all-white [University of Maryland Law School](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Law_School "University of Maryland Law School")—applied to [Howard University School of Law](/wiki/Howard_University_School_of_Law "Howard University School of Law") in Washington, D.C., and was admitted.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 107 At Howard, he was mentored by [Charles Hamilton Houston](/wiki/Charles_Hamilton_Houston "Charles Hamilton Houston"), who taught his students to be "social engineers" willing to use the law as a vehicle to fight for civil rights.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 56[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1499 Marshall graduated in June 1933 ranked first in his class, and he passed the Maryland [bar examination](/wiki/Bar_examination "Bar examination") later that year.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 59, 61

## Legal career

Marshall started a law practice in Baltimore, but it was not financially successful, partially because he spent much of his time working for the benefit of the community.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1499 He volunteered with the Baltimore branch of the [National Association for the Advancement of Colored Persons](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP") (NAACP).[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 477 In 1935, Marshall and Houston brought suit against the University of Maryland on behalf of [Donald Gaines Murray](/wiki/Donald_Gaines_Murray "Donald Gaines Murray"), an African American whose application to the university's law school had been rejected on account of his race.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 78[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 237–238 In that case—*[Murray v. Pearson](/wiki/Murray_v._Pearson "Murray v. Pearson")*—Judge [Eugene O'Dunne](/wiki/Eugene_O%27Dunne "Eugene O'Dunne") ordered that Murray be admitted, and the [Maryland Court of Appeals](/wiki/Maryland_Court_of_Appeals "Maryland Court of Appeals") affirmed, holding that it violated [equal protection](/wiki/Equal_Protection_Clause "Equal Protection Clause") to admit white students to the law school while keeping blacks from being educated in-state.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 231, 246, 256 The decision was never [appealed](/wiki/Appeal "Appeal") to the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") and therefore did not apply nationwide, but it pleased Marshall, who later said that he had filed the lawsuit "to get even with the bastards" who had kept him from attending the school himself.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 47

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/NAACP_leaders_with_poster_NYWTS.jpg/250px-NAACP_leaders_with_poster_NYWTS.jpg)](/wiki/File:NAACP_leaders_with_poster_NYWTS.jpg)

NAACP leaders [Henry L. Moon](/wiki/Henry_L._Moon "Henry L. Moon"), [Roy Wilkins](/wiki/Roy_Wilkins "Roy Wilkins"), [Herbert Hill](/wiki/Herbert_Hill_(labor_director) "Herbert Hill (labor director)"), and Thurgood Marshall in 1956

In 1936, Marshall joined Houston, who had been appointed as the NAACP's special counsel, in New York City, serving as his assistant.[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 477[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 19 They worked together on the landmark case of *[Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada](/wiki/Missouri_ex_rel._Gaines_v._Canada "Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada")* (1938)*.*[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 477 When [Lloyd Lionel Gaines](/wiki/Lloyd_L._Gaines "Lloyd L. Gaines")'s application to the [University of Missouri's law school](/wiki/University_of_Missouri_School_of_Law "University of Missouri School of Law") was rejected on account of his race, he filed suit, arguing that his equal-protection rights had been violated because he had not been provided with a legal education substantially equivalent to that which white students received.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 92–93 After Missouri courts rejected Gaines's claims, Houston—joined by Marshall, who helped to prepare the brief—sought review in the U.S. Supreme Court.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 94[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 70 They did not challenge the Court's decision in *[Plessy v. Ferguson](/wiki/Plessy_v._Ferguson "Plessy v. Ferguson")* (1896), which had accepted the "[separate but equal](/wiki/Separate_but_equal "Separate but equal")" doctrine; instead, they argued that Gaines had been denied an equal education.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 12, 94 In an opinion by Chief Justice [Charles Evans Hughes](/wiki/Charles_Evans_Hughes "Charles Evans Hughes"), the Court held that if Missouri gave whites the opportunity to attend law school in-state, it was required to do the same for blacks.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 70

Houston returned to Washington in 1938, and Marshall assumed his position as special counsel the following year.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 26 He also became the director-counsel of the [NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund Inc.](/wiki/NAACP_Legal_Defense_and_Educational_Fund "NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund") (the Inc Fund), which had been established as a separate organization for tax purposes.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 27 In addition to litigating cases and arguing matters before the Supreme Court, he was responsible for raising money, managing the Inc Fund, and conducting public-relations work.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 27 Marshall litigated a number of cases involving unequal salaries for African Americans, winning nearly all of them; by 1945, he had ended salary disparities in major Southern cities and earned a reputation as a prominent figure in the civil rights movement.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1500 He also defended individuals who had been charged with crimes before both trial courts and the Supreme Court.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1500 Of the thirty-two civil rights cases that Marshall argued before the Supreme Court, he won twenty-nine.[[8]](#cite_note-Routledge-2005-9): 598 He and [W. J. Durham](/wiki/William_J._Durham "William J. Durham") wrote the brief in *[Smith v. Allwright](/wiki/Smith_v._Allwright "Smith v. Allwright")* (1944), in which the Court ruled the [white primary](/wiki/White_primary "White primary") unconstitutional, and he successfully argued both *[Morgan v. Virginia](/wiki/Morgan_v._Virginia "Morgan v. Virginia")* (1946), involving segregation on interstate buses, and a [companion case](/wiki/Companion_case "Companion case") to *[Shelley v. Kraemer](/wiki/Shelley_v._Kraemer "Shelley v. Kraemer")* (1948), involving racially restrictive [covenants](/wiki/Covenant_(law) "Covenant (law)").[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 31–32, 42–43, 53–57

From 1939 to 1947, Marshall was a member of the Board of Directors of the [American Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union"). During that period, he aligned with the faction which favored a more absolutist defense of civil liberties. Most notably, unlike the majority of the Board, he was consistent in his opposition to Roosevelt's [Executive Order 9066](/wiki/Executive_Order_9066 "Executive Order 9066"), which put Japanese Americans into concentration camps. Also, in contrast to most of the Board, Marshall charged that the prosecution of thirty-two right wing opponents of Roosevelt's pre-war foreign policy in the Sedition Trial of 1944 violated the First Amendment.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

In the years after 1945, Marshall resumed his offensive against racial segregation in schools.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1501 Together with his Inc Fund colleagues, he devised a strategy that emphasized the inherent educational disparities caused by segregation rather than the physical differences between the schools provided for blacks and whites.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1501 The Court ruled in Marshall's favor in *[Sipuel v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma](/wiki/Sipuel_v._Board_of_Regents_of_the_University_of_Oklahoma "Sipuel v. Board of Regents of the University of Oklahoma")* (1948), ordering that Oklahoma provide [Ada Lois Sipuel](/wiki/Ada_Lois_Sipuel_Fisher "Ada Lois Sipuel Fisher") with a legal education, although the justices declined to order that she be admitted to the state's law school for whites.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 129–130 In 1950, Marshall brought two cases involving education to the Court: *[McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents](/wiki/McLaurin_v._Oklahoma_State_Regents "McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents")*, which was [George W. McLaurin](/wiki/George_W._McLaurin "George W. McLaurin")'s challenge to unequal treatment at the [University of Oklahoma](/wiki/University_of_Oklahoma "University of Oklahoma")'s graduate school, and *[Sweatt v. Painter](/wiki/Sweatt_v._Painter "Sweatt v. Painter")*, which was [Heman Sweatt](/wiki/Heman_Sweatt "Heman Sweatt")'s challenge to his being required to attend a blacks-only law school in Texas.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 142–145 The Supreme Court ruled in favor of both McLaurin and Sweatt on the same day; although the justices did not overrule *Plessy* and the separate but equal doctrine, they rejected discrimination against African-American students and the provisions of schools for blacks that were inferior to those provided for whites.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 145–146

[![Hayes, Marshall, and Nabit, smiling, stand outside the Supreme Court, with the inscription "Equal Justice Under Law" visible overhead](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/George_Edward_Chalmer_Hayes%2C_Thurgood_Marshall%2C_and_James_Nabrit_in_1954_winning_Brown_case.jpg/250px-George_Edward_Chalmer_Hayes%2C_Thurgood_Marshall%2C_and_James_Nabrit_in_1954_winning_Brown_case.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Edward_Chalmer_Hayes,_Thurgood_Marshall,_and_James_Nabrit_in_1954_winning_Brown_case.jpg)

Marshall (center), [George Edward Chalmer Hayes](/wiki/George_Edward_Chalmer_Hayes "George Edward Chalmer Hayes"), and [James Nabrit](/wiki/James_Nabrit_Jr. "James Nabrit Jr.") congratulate one another after the Supreme Court's decision in *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*.

Marshall next turned to the issue of segregation in primary and secondary schools.[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 478 The NAACP brought suit to challenge segregated schools in Delaware, the District of Columbia, Kansas, South Carolina, and Virginia, arguing both that there were disparities between the physical facilities provided for blacks and whites and that segregation was inherently harmful to African-American children.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1502 Marshall helped to try the South Carolina case.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1502 He called numerous social scientists and other [expert witnesses](/wiki/Expert_witness "Expert witness") to testify regarding the harms of segregation; these included the psychology professor [Ken Clark](/wiki/Kenneth_and_Mamie_Clark#Kenneth_Clark "Kenneth and Mamie Clark"), who testified that segregation in schools caused [self-hatred](/wiki/Self-hatred "Self-hatred") among African-American students and inflicted damage that was "likely to endure as long as the conditions of segregation exist".[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 201–202 The five cases eventually reached the Supreme Court and were argued in December 1952.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 119 In contrast to the oratorical rhetoric of his adversary—[John W. Davis](/wiki/John_W._Davis "John W. Davis"), a former solicitor general and presidential candidate—Marshall spoke plainly and conversationally.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1502 He stated that the only possible justification for segregation "is an inherent determination that the people who were formerly in slavery, regardless of anything else, shall be kept as near that stage as possible. And now is the time, we submit, that this Court should make clear that that is not what our Constitution stands for."[[11]](#cite_note-Rowan-1993-12): 195–196 On May 17, 1954, after internal disagreements and a 1953 reargument, the Supreme Court handed down its unanimous decision in *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")*, holding in an opinion by Chief Justice [Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren") that: "in the field of public education the doctrine of 'separate but equal' has no place. Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal."[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 165, 171, 176, 178 When Marshall heard Warren read those words, he later said, "I was so happy I was numb".[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 226

The Court in *Brown* ordered additional arguments on the proper [remedy](/wiki/Legal_remedy "Legal remedy") for the constitutional violation that it had identified; in [*Brown* II](/wiki/Brown_II "Brown II"), decided in 1955, the justices ordered that desegregation proceed "with all deliberate speed".[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 135–137 Their refusal to set a concrete deadline came as a disappointment to Marshall, who had argued for total integration to be completed by September 1956.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 237[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 478 In the years following the Court's decision, Marshall coordinated challenges to Virginia's "[massive resistance](/wiki/Massive_resistance "Massive resistance")" to *Brown*, and he returned to the Court to successfully argue *[Cooper v. Aaron](/wiki/Cooper_v._Aaron "Cooper v. Aaron")* (1958), involving [Little Rock](/wiki/Little_Rock,_Arkansas "Little Rock, Arkansas")'s attempt to delay integration.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1504 Marshall, who according to the legal scholar [Mark Tushnet](/wiki/Mark_Tushnet "Mark Tushnet") "gradually became a civil rights leader more than a civil rights lawyer", spent substantial amounts of time giving speeches and fundraising;[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1503 in 1960, he accepted an invitation from [Tom Mboya](/wiki/Tom_Mboya "Tom Mboya") to help draft [Kenya's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Kenya_(1963) "Constitution of Kenya (1963)").[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 284–285 By that year, Tushnet writes, he had become "the country's most prominent Supreme Court advocate".[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1505

## Court of Appeals

President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy"), who according to Tushnet "wanted to demonstrate his commitment to the interests of African Americans without incurring enormous political costs", nominated Marshall to be a judge of the [United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") on September 23, 1961.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 9–10 The Second Circuit, which spanned New York, Vermont, and Connecticut, was at the time the nation's prominent appellate court.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 10 When Congress adjourned, Kennedy gave Marshall a [recess appointment](/wiki/Recess_appointment "Recess appointment"), and he took the oath of office on October 23.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 10

Even after his recess appointment, Southern senators continued to delay Marshall's full confirmation for more than eight months.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 181–183 A subcommittee of the [Senate Judiciary Committee](/wiki/Senate_Judiciary_Committee "Senate Judiciary Committee") postponed his hearing several times, leading Senator [Kenneth Keating](/wiki/Kenneth_Keating "Kenneth Keating"), a New York Republican, to charge that the three-member subcommittee, which included two pro-segregation Southern Democrats, was biased against Marshall and engaged in unjustifiable delay.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 298[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 10 The subcommittee held several hearings between May and August 1962; Marshall faced harsh questioning from the Southerners over what the scholar Howard Ball described as "marginal issues at best".[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 182 After further delays from the subcommittee, the full Judiciary Committee bypassed it and, by an 11–4 vote on September 7, endorsed Marshall's nomination.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 12 Following five hours of floor debate, the full Senate confirmed him by a 56–14 vote on September 11, 1962.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 181–183

On the Second Circuit, Marshall authored 98 majority opinions, none of which were reversed by the Supreme Court, as well as 8 concurrences and 12 dissents.[[13]](#cite_note-Daniels-1991-14): 216 He dissented when a majority held in the [Fourth Amendment](/wiki/Fourth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution") case of *United States ex rel. Angelet v. Fay* (1964) that the Supreme Court's 1961 decision in *[Mapp v. Ohio](/wiki/Mapp_v._Ohio "Mapp v. Ohio")* (which held that the [exclusionary rule](/wiki/Exclusionary_rule "Exclusionary rule") applied to the states) did not apply retroactively, writing that the judiciary was "not free to circumscribe the application of a declared constitutional right".[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 184 In *United States v. Wilkins* (1964), he concluded that the [Fifth Amendment](/wiki/Fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution")'s protection against [double jeopardy](/wiki/Double_jeopardy "Double jeopardy") applied to the states; in *People of the State of New York v. Galamison* (1965), he dissented from a ruling upholding the convictions of civil rights protesters at the [New York World's Fair](/wiki/1964_New_York_World%27s_Fair "1964 New York World's Fair").[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 240–241 Marshall's dissents indicated that he favored broader interpretations of constitutional protections than did his colleagues.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 311

## Solicitor General

Marshall's nomination to the office of [Solicitor General](/wiki/Solicitor_General_of_the_United_States "Solicitor General of the United States") was widely viewed as a stepping stone to a Supreme Court appointment.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 19 Johnson pressured Southern senators not to obstruct Marshall's confirmation, and a hearing before a Senate subcommittee lasted only fifteen minutes; the full Senate confirmed him on August 11, 1965.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 251–252[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 190 As Solicitor General, Marshall won fourteen of the nineteen Supreme Court cases he argued.[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 133 He later characterized the position as "the most effective job" and "maybe the best" job he ever had.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 19 Marshall argued in *[Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections](/wiki/Harper_v._Virginia_State_Board_of_Elections "Harper v. Virginia State Board of Elections")* (1966) that conditioning the ability to vote on the payment of a [poll tax](/wiki/Poll_taxes_in_the_United_States "Poll taxes in the United States") was unlawful; in a companion case to *[Miranda v. Arizona](/wiki/Miranda_v._Arizona "Miranda v. Arizona")* (1966), he unsuccessfully maintained on behalf of the government that federal agents were not always required to inform arrested individuals of their rights.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 320, 323 He defended the constitutionality of the [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965") in *[South Carolina v. Katzenbach](/wiki/South_Carolina_v._Katzenbach "South Carolina v. Katzenbach")* (1966) and *[Katzenbach v. Morgan](/wiki/Katzenbach_v._Morgan "Katzenbach v. Morgan")* (1966), winning both cases.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 259–261

## Supreme Court nomination

Main article: [Thurgood Marshall Supreme Court nomination](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_Supreme_Court_nomination "Thurgood Marshall Supreme Court nomination")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13%2C_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13%2C_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thurgood_Marshall_and_President_Lyndon_B._Johnson_June_13,_1967_-_LBJ_Museum_C5706-1_(cropped).jpg)

Marshall meeting with President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") in the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office") of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") on the day that Marshall was nominated by Johnson to serve on the Supreme Court



President Johnson's remarks upon nominating Marshall to the Supreme Court, June 13, 1967



1967 [Universal Newsreel](/wiki/Universal_Newsreel "Universal Newsreel") footage covering Marshall's first day on the Supreme Court

In February 1967, Johnson nominated [Ramsey Clark](/wiki/Ramsey_Clark "Ramsey Clark") to be [Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General").[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 25 The nominee's father was [Tom C. Clark](/wiki/Tom_C._Clark "Tom C. Clark"), an associate justice of the Supreme Court of the United States.[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 150 Fearing that his son's appointment would create substantial [conflicts of interest](/wiki/Conflict_of_interest "Conflict of interest") for him, the elder Clark announced his resignation from the Court.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 25 For Johnson, who had long desired to nominate a non-white justice, the choice of a nominee to fill the ensuing vacancy "was as easy as it was obvious", according to the scholar [Henry J. Abraham](/wiki/Henry_J._Abraham "Henry J. Abraham").[[14]](#cite_note-Abraham-1999-15): 219 Although the President briefly considered selecting [William H. Hastie](/wiki/William_H._Hastie "William H. Hastie") (an African-American appellate judge from Philadelphia) or a female candidate, he decided to choose Marshall.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 25 Johnson announced the nomination in the [White House Rose Garden](/wiki/White_House_Rose_Garden "White House Rose Garden") on June 13, declaring that Marshall "deserves the appointment ... I believe that it is the right thing to do, the right time to do it, the right man and the right place."[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 151[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 25

The public received the nomination favorably, and Marshall was praised by prominent senators from both parties.[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 151, 153 The [Senate Judiciary Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_the_Judiciary "United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary") held hearings for five days in July.[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 153 Marshall faced harsh criticism from such senators as Mississippi's [James O. Eastland](/wiki/James_O._Eastland "James O. Eastland"), North Carolina's [Sam Ervin Jr.](/wiki/Sam_Ervin_Jr. "Sam Ervin Jr."), Arkansas's [John McClellan](/wiki/John_L._McClellan "John L. McClellan"), and South Carolina's [Strom Thurmond](/wiki/Strom_Thurmond "Strom Thurmond"), all of whom opposed the nominee's liberal jurisprudence.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 195 In what [*Time* magazine](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") characterized as a "Yahoo-type hazing", Thurmond asked Marshall over sixty questions about various minor aspects of the history of certain constitutional provisions.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 196 By an 11–5 vote on August 3, the committee recommended that Marshall be confirmed.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 337 On August 30, after six hours of debate, senators voted 69–11[[b]](#cite_note-16) to confirm Marshall to the Supreme Court.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 197[[15]](#cite_note-17) He took the constitutional oath of office on October 2, 1967, becoming the first African American to serve as a justice of the Supreme Court of the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 338

## Supreme Court

[![Photograph of Marshall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Thurgoodmarshall1967_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Thurgoodmarshall1967_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thurgoodmarshall1967_(cropped).jpg)

Marshall, 1967

Marshall remained on the Supreme Court for nearly twenty-four years, serving until his retirement in 1991.[[7]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1994-8): 314 The Court to which he was appointed—the [Warren Court](/wiki/Warren_Court "Warren Court")—had a consistent liberal majority, and Marshall's jurisprudence was similar to that of its leaders, Chief Justice Warren and Justice [William J. Brennan Jr.](/wiki/William_J._Brennan_Jr. "William J. Brennan Jr.")[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1507 Although he wrote few major opinions during this period due to his lack of seniority, he was typically in the majority.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 344[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 335 As a result of four Supreme Court appointments by President [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), however, the liberal coalition vanished.[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 335 The Court under Chief Justice [Warren Burger](/wiki/Warren_E._Burger "Warren E. Burger") (the [Burger Court](/wiki/Burger_Court "Burger Court")) was not as conservative as some observers had anticipated, but the task of constructing liberal majorities case-by-case was left primarily to Brennan; Marshall's most consequential contributions to constitutional law came in dissent.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1508 The justice left much of his work to his [law clerks](/wiki/Law_clerk "Law clerk"), preferring to determine the outcome of the case and then allow the clerks to draft the opinion themselves.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 215 He took umbrage at frequent claims that he did no work and spent his time watching daytime [soap operas](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera");[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 203 according to Tushnet, who clerked for Marshall, the idea that he "was a lazy Justice uninterested in the Court's work ... is wrong and perhaps racist".[[17]](#cite_note-19): 2109 Marshall's closest colleague and friend on the Court was Brennan,[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 210–211 and the two justices agreed so often that their clerks privately referred to them as "Justice Brennanmarshall".[[c]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22): 10 He also had a high regard for Warren, whom he described as "probably the greatest Chief Justice who ever lived".[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 210

Marshall consistently sided with the Supreme Court's liberal bloc.[[20]](#cite_note-23): 347 According to the scholar William J. Daniels: "His approach to justice was Warren Court–style [legal realism](/wiki/Legal_realism "Legal realism") ... In his dissenting opinions he emphasized individual rights, fundamental fairness, equal opportunity and protection under the law, the supremacy of the Constitution as the embodiment of rights and privileges, and the Supreme Court's responsibility to play a significant role in giving meaning to the notion of constitutional rights."[[13]](#cite_note-Daniels-1991-14): 234–235 Marshall's jurisprudence was pragmatic and relied on his real-world experience as a lawyer and as an African American.[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 339 He disagreed with the notion (favored by some of his conservative colleagues) that the Constitution [should be interpreted according to the Founders' original understandings](/wiki/Originalism "Originalism");[[21]](#cite_note-Hall-2001-24): 382 in a 1987 speech commemorating the Constitution's bicentennial, he said:[[22]](#cite_note-25): 2, 5

> ... I do not believe that the meaning of the Constitution was forever "fixed" at the Philadelphia Convention. Nor do I find the wisdom, foresight, and sense of justice exhibited by the framers particularly profound. To the contrary, the government they devised was defective from the start, requiring several amendments, a civil war, and momentous social transformation to attain the system of constitutional government, and its respect for the individual freedoms and human rights, that we hold as fundamental today ... "We the People" no longer enslave, but the credit does not belong to the framers. It belongs to those who refused to acquiesce in outdated notions of "liberty", "justice", and "equality", and who strived to better them ... I plan to celebrate the bicentennial of the Constitution as a living document, including the Bill of Rights and the other amendments protecting individual freedoms and human rights.

### Equal protection and civil rights

[![Black-and-white photograph of the nine justices of the Supreme Court in their judicial robes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/US_Supreme_Court_1976.png/500px-US_Supreme_Court_1976.png)](/wiki/File:US_Supreme_Court_1976.png)

Justices of the Supreme Court of the United States, 1976. Marshall is in the bottom row, first from the right.

As the Court became increasingly conservative, Marshall found himself dissenting in numerous cases regarding racial discrimination.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1511 When the majority held in *[Milliken v. Bradley](/wiki/Milliken_v._Bradley "Milliken v. Bradley")* that a lower court had gone too far in ordering [busing](/wiki/Desegregation_busing "Desegregation busing") to reduce racial imbalances between schools in Detroit, he dissented, criticizing his colleagues for what he viewed as a lack of resolve to implement desegregation even when faced with difficulties and public resistance.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 344–345 In a dissent in *[City of Memphis v. Greene](/w/index.php?title=City_of_Memphis_v._Greene&action=edit&redlink=1 "City of Memphis v. Greene (page does not exist)")* that according to Tushnet "demonstrated his sense of the practical reality that formed the context for abstract legal issues", he argued that a street closure that made it more difficult for residents of an African-American neighborhood to reach a city park was unconstitutional because it sent "a plain and powerful symbolic message" to blacks "that because of their race, they are to stay out of the all-white enclave ... and should instead take the long way around".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 91–92 Marshall felt that [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action") was both necessary and constitutional;[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 257 in an opinion in *[Regents of the University of California v. Bakke](/wiki/Regents_of_the_University_of_California_v._Bakke "Regents of the University of California v. Bakke")*, he commented that it was "more than a little ironic that, after several hundred years of class-based discrimination against Negroes, the Court is unwilling to hold that a class-based remedy for that discrimination is permissible".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 131 Dissenting in *[City of Richmond v. J.A. Croson Co.](/wiki/City_of_Richmond_v._J.A._Croson_Co. "City of Richmond v. J.A. Croson Co.")*, he rejected the majority's decision to strike down an affirmative-action program for government contractors, stating that he did "not believe that this Nation is anywhere close to eradicating racial discrimination or its vestiges".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 139–143

Marshall's most influential contribution to constitutional doctrine was his "sliding-scale" approach to the Equal Protection Clause, which posited that the judiciary should assess a law's constitutionality by balancing its goals against its impact on groups and rights.[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 336 Dissenting in *[Dandridge v. Williams](/wiki/Dandridge_v._Williams "Dandridge v. Williams")*, a case in which the majority upheld Maryland's $250-a-month cap on welfare payments against claims that it was insufficient for large families, he argued that [rational basis review](/wiki/Rational_basis_review "Rational basis review") was not appropriate in cases involving "the literally vital interests of a powerless minority".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 98–99 In what [Cass Sunstein](/wiki/Cass_Sunstein "Cass Sunstein") described as the justice's greatest opinion, Marshall dissented when the Court in *[San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez](/wiki/San_Antonio_Independent_School_District_v._Rodriguez "San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez")* upheld a system in which local schools were funded mainly through property taxes, arguing that the policy (which meant that poorer school districts obtained less money than richer ones) resulted in unconstitutional discrimination.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 224–225[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 100–101 His dissent in *[Harris v. McRae](/wiki/Harris_v._McRae "Harris v. McRae")*, in which the Court upheld the [Hyde Amendment](/wiki/Hyde_Amendment "Hyde Amendment")'s ban on the use of [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid") funds to pay for [abortions](/wiki/Abortions "Abortions"), rebuked the majority for applying a "relentlessly formalistic catechism" that failed to take account of the amendment's "crushing burden on indigent women".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 102–103 Although Marshall's sliding-scale approach was never adopted by the Court as a whole, the legal scholar [Susan Low Bloch](/wiki/Susan_Low_Bloch "Susan Low Bloch") comments that "his consistent criticism seems to have prodded the Court to somewhat greater flexibility".[[23]](#cite_note-26): 527

### Criminal procedure and capital punishment

Marshall supported the Warren Court's constitutional decisions on criminal law, and he wrote the opinion of the Court in *[Benton v. Maryland](/wiki/Benton_v._Maryland "Benton v. Maryland")*, which held that the Constitution's prohibition of double jeopardy applied to the states.[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 337 After the retirements of Warren and Justice [Hugo Black](/wiki/Hugo_Black "Hugo Black"), however, "Marshall was continually shocked at the refusal" of the Burger and [Rehnquist Courts](/wiki/Rehnquist_Court "Rehnquist Court") "to hold police and those involved in the criminal justice system responsible for acting according to the language and the spirit of fundamental procedural guarantees", according to Ball.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 286 He favored a strict interpretation of the Fourth Amendment's warrant requirement and opposed rulings that made exceptions to that provision;[[24]](#cite_note-Ogletree-1989-27): 112 in *[United States v. Ross](/wiki/United_States_v._Ross "United States v. Ross")*, for instance, he indignantly dissented when the Court upheld a conviction that was based on evidence discovered during a warrantless search of containers that had been found in an automobile.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 291–292 Marshall felt strongly that the [*Miranda* doctrine](/wiki/Miranda_warning "Miranda warning") should be expanded and fully enforced.[[24]](#cite_note-Ogletree-1989-27): 112 In cases involving the [Sixth Amendment](/wiki/Sixth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Sixth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), he argued that defendants must have competent attorneys; dissenting in *[Strickland v. Washington](/wiki/Strickland_v._Washington "Strickland v. Washington")*, Marshall (parting ways with Brennan) rejected the majority's conclusion that defendants must prove prejudice in [ineffective assistance of counsel](/wiki/Ineffective_assistance_of_counsel "Ineffective assistance of counsel") cases.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 187–188[[24]](#cite_note-Ogletree-1989-27): 112

Marshall fervently opposed [capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States") throughout his time on the Court, arguing that it was [cruel and unusual](/wiki/Cruel_and_unusual_punishment "Cruel and unusual punishment") and therefore unconstitutional under the [Eighth Amendment](/wiki/Eighth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Eighth Amendment to the United States Constitution").[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 318 He was the only justice with considerable experience defending those charged with capital crimes, and he expressed concern about the fact that injustices in death-penalty cases could not be remedied, often commenting: "Death is so lasting."[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1514–1515 In *[Furman v. Georgia](/wiki/Furman_v._Georgia "Furman v. Georgia")*, a case in which the Court struck down the capital-punishment statutes that were in force at the time, Marshall wrote that the death penalty was "morally unacceptable to the people of the United States at this time in their history" and that it "falls upon the poor, the ignorant, and the underprivileged members of society".[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1515 When the Court in *[Gregg v. Georgia](/wiki/Gregg_v._Georgia "Gregg v. Georgia")* upheld new death-penalty laws that required juries to consider [aggravating](/wiki/Aggravation_(law) "Aggravation (law)") and [mitigating circumstances](/wiki/Mitigating_factor "Mitigating factor"), he dissented, describing capital punishment as a "vestigial savagery" that was immoral and violative of the Eighth Amendment.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 305 Afterwards, Marshall and Brennan dissented in every instance in which the Court declined to review a death sentence, filing more than 1,400 dissents that read: "Adhering to our views that the death penalty is in all circumstances cruel and unusual punishment prohibited by the Eighth and Fourteenth Amendments, we would grant [certiorari](/wiki/Certiorari "Certiorari") and vacate the death sentence in this case."[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 175

### First Amendment

According to Ball, Marshall felt that the rights protected by the [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") were the Constitution's most important principles and that they could be restricted only for extremely compelling reasons.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 316 In a 1969 opinion in *[Stanley v. Georgia](/wiki/Stanley_v._Georgia "Stanley v. Georgia")*, he held that it was unconstitutional to criminalize the possession of [obscene material](/wiki/Obscenity "Obscenity").[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 335 For the Court, he reversed the conviction of a Georgia man charged with possessing pornography, writing: "If the First Amendment means anything, it means that a State has no business telling a man, sitting alone in his own house, what books he may read or what films he may watch."[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 317 In *[Amalgamated Food Employees Union Local 400 v. Logan Valley Plaza](/w/index.php?title=Amalgamated_Food_Employees_Union_Local_400_v._Logan_Valley_Plaza&action=edit&redlink=1 "Amalgamated Food Employees Union Local 400 v. Logan Valley Plaza (page does not exist)")*, he wrote for the Court that protesters had the right to picket on private property that was open to the public—a decision that was effectively overruled (over Marshall's dissent) four years later in [*Lloyd Corporation v. Tanner*](/wiki/Lloyd_Corp._v._Tanner "Lloyd Corp. v. Tanner").[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 323–324 He emphasized equality in his free speech opinions, writing in *[Chicago Police Dept. v. Mosley](/wiki/Chicago_Police_Dept._v._Mosley "Chicago Police Dept. v. Mosley")* that "above all else, the First Amendment means that government has no power to restrict expression because of its messages, its ideas, its subject matter, or its content".[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1513 Making comparisons to earlier civil rights protests, Marshall vigorously dissented in *[Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence](/wiki/Clark_v._Community_for_Creative_Non-Violence "Clark v. Community for Creative Non-Violence")*, a case in which the Court ruled that the government could forbid homeless individuals from protesting poverty by sleeping overnight in [Lafayette Park](/wiki/Lafayette_Park_(Washington,_D.C.) "Lafayette Park (Washington, D.C.)"); although Burger decried their claims as "frivolous" attempts to "trivialize" the Constitution, Marshall argued that the protesters were engaged in constitutionally protected [symbolic speech](/wiki/Symbolic_speech "Symbolic speech").[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 378[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 326–327

Marshall joined the majority in *[Texas v. Johnson](/wiki/Texas_v._Johnson "Texas v. Johnson")* and *[United States v. Eichman](/wiki/United_States_v._Eichman "United States v. Eichman")*, two cases in which the Court held that the First Amendment protected the right to burn the American flag.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 332–333 He favored the total [separation of church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state "Separation of church and state"), dissenting when the Court upheld in *[Lynch v. Donnelly](/wiki/Lynch_v._Donnelly "Lynch v. Donnelly")* a city's display of a [nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene") and joining the majority in *[Wallace v. Jaffree](/wiki/Wallace_v._Jaffree "Wallace v. Jaffree")* to strike down an Alabama law regarding prayer in schools.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 343–346 On the issue of the [free exercise of religion](/wiki/Free_Exercise_Clause "Free Exercise Clause"), Marshall voted with the majority in *[Wisconsin v. Yoder](/wiki/Wisconsin_v._Yoder "Wisconsin v. Yoder")* to hold that a school attendance law could not be constitutionally applied to the [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish"), and he joined Justice [Harry Blackmun](/wiki/Harry_Blackmun "Harry Blackmun")'s dissent when the Court in *[Employment Division v. Smith](/wiki/Employment_Division_v._Smith "Employment Division v. Smith")* upheld a restriction on religious uses of [peyote](/wiki/Peyote "Peyote") and curtailed *[Sherbert v. Verner](/wiki/Sherbert_v._Verner "Sherbert v. Verner")*'s [strict scrutiny](/wiki/Strict_scrutiny "Strict scrutiny") standard.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 351–353 In the view of [J. Clay Smith Jr.](/wiki/J._Clay_Smith_Jr. "J. Clay Smith Jr.") and Scott Burrell, the justice was "an unyielding supporter of civil liberties", whose "commitment to the values of the First Amendment was enhanced from actually realizing the historical consequences of being on the weaker and poorer side of power".[[25]](#cite_note-28): 477

### Privacy

In Marshall's view, the Constitution guaranteed to all citizens the [right to privacy](/wiki/Right_to_privacy "Right to privacy"); he felt that although the Constitution nowhere mentioned such a right expressly, it could be inferred from various provisions of the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights").[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 356 He joined the majority in *[Eisenstadt v. Baird](/wiki/Eisenstadt_v._Baird "Eisenstadt v. Baird")* to strike down a statute that prohibited the distribution or sale of [contraceptives](/wiki/Contraceptives "Contraceptives") to unmarried persons, dissented when the Court in *[Bowers v. Hardwick](/wiki/Bowers_v._Hardwick "Bowers v. Hardwick")* upheld an [anti-sodomy law](/wiki/Sodomy_law "Sodomy law"), and dissented from the majority's decision in *[Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Department of Health](/wiki/Cruzan_v._Director,_Missouri_Department_of_Health "Cruzan v. Director, Missouri Department of Health")* that the Constitution did not protect an unconditional [right to die](/wiki/Right_to_die "Right to die").[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 358–364 On the issue of abortion rights, the author [Carl T. Rowan](/wiki/Carl_T._Rowan "Carl T. Rowan") comments that "no justice ever supported a woman's right to choice as uncompromisingly as Marshall did".[[11]](#cite_note-Rowan-1993-12): 323 He joined Blackmun's opinion for the Court in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")*, which held that the Constitution protected a woman's right to have an abortion,[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 342 and he consistently voted against state laws that sought to limit that right in cases such as *[Maher v. Roe](/wiki/Maher_v._Roe "Maher v. Roe")*, *[H. L. v. Matheson](/wiki/H._L._v._Matheson "H. L. v. Matheson")*, *[Akron v. Akron Center for Reproductive Health](/wiki/Akron_v._Akron_Center_for_Reproductive_Health "Akron v. Akron Center for Reproductive Health")*, *[Thornburgh v. American College of Obstetricians & Gynecologists](/wiki/Thornburgh_v._American_College_of_Obstetricians_%26_Gynecologists "Thornburgh v. American College of Obstetricians & Gynecologists")*, and *[Webster v. Reproductive Health Services](/wiki/Webster_v._Reproductive_Health_Services "Webster v. Reproductive Health Services")*.[[26]](#cite_note-29): 203

### Other topics

During his service on the Supreme Court, Marshall participated in over 3,400 cases and authored 322 majority opinions.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 401 He was a member of the unanimous majority in *[United States v. Nixon](/wiki/United_States_v._Nixon "United States v. Nixon")* that rejected President Nixon's claims of absolute [executive privilege](/wiki/Executive_privilege "Executive privilege").[[27]](#cite_note-30): 78 Marshall wrote several influential decisions in the fields of [corporate law](/wiki/Corporate_law "Corporate law") and [securities law](/wiki/Securities_law "Securities law"), including a frequently-cited opinion regarding [materiality](/wiki/Materiality_(law)#In_corporate_and_securities_law "Materiality (law)") in *[TSC Industries, Inc. v. Northway, Inc.](/wiki/TSC_Industries,_Inc._v._Northway,_Inc. "TSC Industries, Inc. v. Northway, Inc.")*[[28]](#cite_note-31): 25 His opinions involving [personal jurisdiction](/wiki/Personal_jurisdiction "Personal jurisdiction"), such as *[Shaffer v. Heitner](/wiki/Shaffer_v._Heitner "Shaffer v. Heitner")*, were pragmatic and de-emphasized the importance of state boundaries.[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1514 According to Tushnet, Marshall was "the Court's liberal specialist in [Native American law](/wiki/Native_American_Indian_law "Native American Indian law")"; he endeavored to protect Native Americans from regulatory action on the part of the states.[[16]](#cite_note-Tushnet-2006-18): 338 He favored a rigid interpretation of procedural requirements, saying in one case that "rules mean what they say"—a position that in Tushnet's view was motivated by the justice's "traditionalist streak".[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 185–186

Like most Supreme Court justices, many of Marshall's [law clerks](/wiki/Law_clerk "Law clerk") went on to become prominent lawyers and legal scholars. His clerks included future Supreme Court justice [Elena Kagan](/wiki/Elena_Kagan "Elena Kagan"), U.S. circuit judge [Douglas H. Ginsburg](/wiki/Douglas_H._Ginsburg "Douglas H. Ginsburg"), and legal scholars [Cass Sunstein](/wiki/Cass_Sunstein "Cass Sunstein"), [Mark Tushnet](/wiki/Mark_Tushnet "Mark Tushnet"), and [Martha Minow](/wiki/Martha_Minow "Martha Minow").

## Personal life

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Thurgood_Marshall_and_family%2C_1965.png/250px-Thurgood_Marshall_and_family%2C_1965.png)](/wiki/File:Thurgood_Marshall_and_family,_1965.png)

Marshall, his wife [Cissy](/wiki/Cecilia_Suyat_Marshall "Cecilia Suyat Marshall"), and their children [John](/wiki/John_W._Marshall "John W. Marshall") (bottom left) and [Thurgood Jr.](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_Jr. "Thurgood Marshall Jr.") (bottom right), 1965

Marshall wed [Vivian "Buster" Burey](/wiki/Vivian_Burey_Marshall "Vivian Burey Marshall") on September 4, 1929, while he was a student at Lincoln University.[[3]](#cite_note-Gibson-2012-4): 101, 103 They remained married until her death from cancer in 1955.[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 180 Marshall married [Cecilia "Cissy" Suyat](/wiki/Cecilia_Suyat_Marshall "Cecilia Suyat Marshall"), an NAACP secretary, eleven months later; they had two children: [Thurgood Jr.](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_Jr. "Thurgood Marshall Jr.") and [John](/wiki/John_W._Marshall "John W. Marshall").[[2]](#cite_note-Davis-1992-3): 180–181 Thurgood Jr. became an attorney and worked in the [Clinton administration](/wiki/Clinton_administration "Clinton administration"), and John directed the [U.S. Marshals Service](/wiki/United_States_Marshals_Service "United States Marshals Service") and served as [Virginia's secretary of public safety](/wiki/Virginia_Secretary_of_Public_Safety "Virginia Secretary of Public Safety").[[29]](#cite_note-32)

Marshall was an active member of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") and served as a delegate to its 1964 convention, walking out after a resolution to recognize a right to disobey immoral segregation laws was voted down.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 180 He was a [Prince Hall Mason](/wiki/Prince_Hall_Freemasonry "Prince Hall Freemasonry"), attending meetings and participating in rituals.[[12]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997-13): 180 He refused to attend the Supreme Court's annual Christmas party believing that it infringed upon the separation of church and state.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 343

Justice [Sandra Day O'Connor](/wiki/Sandra_Day_O%27Connor "Sandra Day O'Connor"), who served with Marshall on the Supreme Court for a decade, wrote that "it was rare during our conference deliberations that he would not share an anecdote, a joke or a story"; although O'Connor initially treated the stories as "welcome diversions", she later "realized that behind most of the anecdotes was a relevant legal point".[[30]](#cite_note-33): 1217–1218

## Retirement, later life, and death

[![Gravestone reading "Thurgood Marshall / Associate Justice / 1967–1991 / United States Supreme Court / July 2, 1908 – January 24, 1993](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Thurgood_Marshall%2C_First_African-American_Supreme_Court_Justice.jpg/250px-Thurgood_Marshall%2C_First_African-American_Supreme_Court_Justice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thurgood_Marshall,_First_African-American_Supreme_Court_Justice.jpg)

Marshall's gravestone at [Arlington National Cemetery](/wiki/Arlington_National_Cemetery "Arlington National Cemetery")

Marshall did not wish to retire—he frequently said "I was appointed to a life term, and I intend to serve it"—but he had been in ill health for many years, and Brennan's retirement in 1990 left him unhappy and isolated on the Court.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 377–378[[31]](#cite_note-Atkinson-1999-34): 156, 158 The 82-year-old justice announced on June 27, 1991, that he would retire.[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 480 When asked at a [press conference](/wiki/Press_conference "Press conference") what was wrong with him that would cause him to leave the Court, he replied: "What's *wrong* with me? I'm old. I'm getting old and coming apart!"[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 379

President [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") (whom Marshall loathed) nominated [Clarence Thomas](/wiki/Clarence_Thomas "Clarence Thomas"), who had served in the Reagan and Bush administrations, to replace Marshall.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 379 His retirement took effect on October 1.[[32]](#cite_note-35): 951

Marshall served as a [visiting judge](/wiki/Visiting_judge "Visiting judge") on the Second Circuit for a week in January 1992, and he received the [American Bar Association](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association")'s highest award in August of that year.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 394–395 His health continued to deteriorate, and, on January 24, 1993, at the [Bethesda Naval Medical Center](/wiki/Bethesda_Naval_Medical_Center "Bethesda Naval Medical Center"), he died of [heart failure](/wiki/Heart_failure "Heart failure").[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 395[[31]](#cite_note-Atkinson-1999-34): 159 He was 84 years old.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 396

Marshall [lay in repose](/wiki/Lying_in_repose "Lying in repose") in the Great Hall of the Supreme Court,[[31]](#cite_note-Atkinson-1999-34): 159 and thousands thronged there to pay their respects;[[6]](#cite_note-Bloch-1993-7): 480 more than four thousand attended his funeral service at the [Washington National Cathedral](/wiki/Washington_National_Cathedral "Washington National Cathedral").[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 397 The civil rights leader [Vernon E. Jordan](/wiki/Vernon_E._Jordan "Vernon E. Jordan") said that Marshall had "demonstrat[ed] that the law could be an instrument of liberation", while Chief Justice [William Rehnquist](/wiki/William_Rehnquist "William Rehnquist") gave a eulogy in which he said: "Inscribed above the front entrance to the Supreme Court building are the words 'Equal justice under law'. Surely no one individual did more to make these words a reality than Thurgood Marshall."[[33]](#cite_note-36) Marshall was buried at [Arlington National Cemetery](/wiki/Arlington_National_Cemetery "Arlington National Cemetery").[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 398

## Appraisal and legacy

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Marshall-courthouse1.jpg/250px-Marshall-courthouse1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marshall-courthouse1.jpg)

The [Thurgood Marshall United States Courthouse](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_United_States_Courthouse "Thurgood Marshall United States Courthouse"), renamed in Marshall's honor in 2001

According to the scholar Daniel Moak, Marshall "profoundly shaped the political direction of the United States", "transformed constitutional law", and "opened up new facets of citizenship to black Americans".[[34]](#cite_note-37): 411 For Tushnet, he was "probably the most important American lawyer of the twentieth century";[[5]](#cite_note-Tushnet-1997a-6): 1498 in the view of the political scientist [Robert C. Smith](/wiki/Robert_C._Smith_(political_scientist) "Robert C. Smith (political scientist)"), he was "one of the greatest leaders in the history of the African-American struggle for freedom and equality".[[35]](#cite_note-38): 218 A 1999 survey of black political scientists listed Marshall as one of the ten greatest African-American leaders in history; panelists described him as the "greatest jurist of the twentieth century" and stated that he "spearheaded the creation of the legal foundations of the civil rights movement".[[36]](#cite_note-39): 129, 132 Scholars of the Supreme Court have not rated Marshall as highly as some of his colleagues: although his pre–Supreme Court legal career and his staunch liberalism have met with broad approval, a perception that he lacked substantial influence over his fellow justices has harmed his reputation.[[37]](#cite_note-Ross-1996-40): 407–408, 439 In Abraham's view, "he was one of America's greatest public lawyers, but he was not a great Supreme Court justice".[[14]](#cite_note-Abraham-1999-15): 222 A 1993 survey of legal scholars found that Marshall was ranked as the seventeenth-greatest justice of the Supreme Court—a rating that, while still lower than that of his fellow liberal justices, was substantially higher than was recorded in an earlier survey.[[37]](#cite_note-Ross-1996-40): 408

Marshall has received numerous tributes.[[38]](#cite_note-Gilmore-2008-41): 20 The state of Maryland renamed Baltimore's airport the [Baltimore/Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport](/wiki/Baltimore/Washington_International_Airport "Baltimore/Washington International Airport") in 2005, and the University of Maryland's [law library](/wiki/Law_library "Law library") is named in his honor.[[38]](#cite_note-Gilmore-2008-41): 20[[39]](#cite_note-42): 617 Buildings named for Marshall include New York's 590-foot-high [Thurgood Marshall United States Courthouse](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_United_States_Courthouse "Thurgood Marshall United States Courthouse") (renamed in 2001), where he heard cases as an appellate judge, and [the federal judicial center](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_Federal_Judiciary_Building "Thurgood Marshall Federal Judiciary Building") in Washington.[[40]](#cite_note-Williams-2017-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44): 859–860 He is the namesake of streets and schools throughout the nation, including notably [Thurgood Marshall College](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall_College "Thurgood Marshall College") at the [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego").[[38]](#cite_note-Gilmore-2008-41): 20 Marshall posthumously received the [Presidential Medal of Freedom](/wiki/Presidential_Medal_of_Freedom "Presidential Medal of Freedom") from President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") in 1993,[[42]](#cite_note-45): 253 and the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") issued a [commemorative stamp](/wiki/Commemorative_stamp "Commemorative stamp") in his honor in 2003.[[43]](#cite_note-46) He was depicted by [Sidney Poitier](/wiki/Sidney_Poitier "Sidney Poitier") in the 1991 television movie [*Separate but Equal*](/wiki/Separate_but_Equal_(film) "Separate but Equal (film)"),[[44]](#cite_note-47): 335 by [Laurence Fishburne](/wiki/Laurence_Fishburne "Laurence Fishburne") in [George Stevens Jr.](/wiki/George_Stevens_Jr. "George Stevens Jr.")'s Broadway play [*Thurgood*](/wiki/Thurgood_(play) "Thurgood (play)"),[[45]](#cite_note-48) and by [Chadwick Boseman](/wiki/Chadwick_Boseman "Chadwick Boseman") in the 2017 film [*Marshall*](/wiki/Marshall_(film) "Marshall (film)").[[40]](#cite_note-Williams-2017-43)

In 2006, the Episcopal Diocese of Washington proposed naming Marshall a [saint](/wiki/Saints_in_Anglicanism "Saints in Anglicanism") and adding a feast day honoring him to the [Episcopal liturgical calendar](/wiki/The_Calendar_of_the_Church_Year "The Calendar of the Church Year").[[46]](#cite_note-49) May 17 was selected as the Feast of Thurgood Marshall to mark the [Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education") Supreme Court decision.[[47]](#cite_note-50) His feast day was included among the [Lesser Feasts and Fasts](/wiki/Lesser_Feasts_and_Fasts "Lesser Feasts and Fasts") on a trial basis in 2009, and he was made a permanent saint of the church in 2018.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Marshall was originally named "Thoroughgood" (his paternal grandfather's name), but he changed it to the briefer "Thurgood" when he was in the second grade.[[1]](#cite_note-Ball-1998-1): 13
2. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Thirty-two Republicans and thirty-seven Democrats voted to confirm Marshall; one Republican (Thurmond) and ten Southern Democrats voted against him.[[9]](#cite_note-Bland-1993-10): 156 On the urging of Johnson, twenty Southerners did not cast a vote.[[4]](#cite_note-Williams-1998-5): 337
3. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** In non-unanimous cases decided by an eight- or nine-justice court, Marshall and Brennan voted the same way 91.67% of the time during the Warren Court, 87.33% of the time during the Burger Court, and 94.86% of the time during the Rehnquist Court.[[18]](#cite_note-20): 638, 642, 646
